3: Globally, Brazil and France are among the largest producers and consumers of chicken meat. Brazil recently overtook France in the export of chickens, and the Brazilian poultry sector is fully expanding, increasing the number of chicken farms in several re- gions of the country. At the same time, the chicken industry in France is in decline due to competition with emerging economies that offer the product at a lower price to European and Middle Eastern markets (Guémené and Lescoat, 2007). The differences in human and natural resources, climate, topography and the geographic distribution of the agricultural production systems of
Brazil and France have led to the establishment of different supply chains, which may result in different environmental impacts.

In southern Brazil, a traditional region of pig and poultry pro- duction (Spies, 2003), both the government and the population are increasingly concerned about the environmental impacts of these activities. Today, Brazilian poultry is also produced in the Center- West region of the country, a region with large farms that are specialized in the production of maize and soybeans and with a lower density of pig and poultry production at the regional scale.

In France, given the size of the farms and their regional density, poultry production is subject to rules that define acceptable levels of field application of nitrogen (N) and, more recently, phosphorus (P) (Magdelaine, 2008). Another major issue in France is the contribution of the poultry sector to greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions. The increased concern with environmental problems may lead to a significant increase in costs in the poultry sector (Magdelaine and Chesnel, 2005). This creates a striking contrast: the potential to intensify or expand agricultural production in many areas of Brazil, with a poultry sector that is fully expanding, versus

many sectors competing for a limited area in France, with a poultry industry in decline due to competition with emerging countries. Poultry production is a typical example of globalization, as it is easy to outsource and uses inputs from all over the world. This generates intense competition among poultry-producing regions. The growth of global poultry production is a significant phenomenon in food production that deserves a full assessment of the entire product life cycle to identify its environmental impacts, although in this study we did not analyze the distribution and consumption phases.

This study compares the environmental burdens of four broiler chicken production systems, two from Brazil and two from France, using a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) "cradle to gate" approach. Table 1 summarizes the systems, their locations and acronyms adopted.

This paper seeks to assess the environmental impacts of pro- cessed whole chicken, packed and cooled at the slaughterhouse gate. However, to help understand the environmental impacts of the agricultural sector, we present the results both per kg of live weight (LW) and per unit of economic value (Euro) at the farm gate.

This paper seeks to assess the environmental impacts of pro- cessed whole chicken, packed and cooled at the slaughterhouse gate. 6: The LCA for the four systems studied begins with the production of crop inputs and passes through the phases of crop production; grain drying and processing; feed manufacturing; broiler breeder production; chick production; chicken rearing; and slaughter, cooling and packaging of whole chicken, including all transport phases, up to the slaughterhouse exit gate (Fig. 1). The production and maintenance of chicken houses and slaughterhouse buildings and machines were not included. We considered that a portion of the crops is produced using chicken manure coming from studied systems. Thus, some chemical fertilizer is no longer used, slightly decreasing the estimated impacts.
Data about feed composition, inputs used in the crop stage and yields considered are shown in Supplementary material, Tables S1 and S2.

Inventory data for deforestation, crop production and transport in Brazil were based on Prudêncio da Silva (2011). For French crops, data on production practices came from a survey of agricultural practices (AGRESTE, 2006). Yields used were means for the period 2004-2007. Inventory data for grain drying, soybean processing, transport distances, and chicken production and slaughter in Brazil were obtained from EPAGRI (the Santa Catarina State Institution for Agricultural Research), EMBRAPA (the Federal Institution for Agri- cultural Research), AURORA (a poultry and pig production cooper- ative) and interviews with local agricultural advisors. In France, inventory data for feed production were obtained from INRA UMR SAS (Rennes), Maîsadour grain and poultry production company) and interviews with local agricultural advisors.

Since we focused on the chicken-production stage, we assumed the same number of breeders (2.2) per tonne of chicken LW in all systems studied. The coproducts of slaughtering (meal and grease) are used to produce animal feedstock; thus, we considered that they leave the system.
9: Based on technical performance indicators (Table 2), all four systems were considered intensive except for Label Rouge, which is a method for producing high-quality chicken based on official specifications approved by French public authorities. These speci- fications require the use of slow-growing poultry strains that pro- duce meat with superior taste. Label Rouge chickens must not be slaughtered before the age of 81 days, have low population density in the building (max. 11 chickens/m²), have access to an outdoor run (2 /chicken) and be fed at least 75% cereals by mass.

This study was based on data that are representative of certain areas, not on data from specific farms. Thus, although the standard systems used rapid-growth strains and the Label Rouge system used slow-growth strains, it was not possible to determine exactly which strains were used.

To perform the LCAs of chicken production, we did not use rearing time as input data. We used only feed-conversion rate, mortality rate, carcass yield and, to estimate gaseous emissions, final weight.
10: Emissions from crop production are highly variable and depend on factors such as climate, soil type and farming practices. The emissions considered were ammonia (NH3) to air from chemical fertilizers, animal manure and slurry, nitrate (NO) and PO to water, N20 and NO to air and heavy metals to soil. Regarding the CO2 and energy contained in crops, there is general agreement, as expressed by Williams et al. (2006), that under steady-state con- ditions, nearly all C in the crop will rapidly (within 1-2 years) be emitted to the atmosphere. We therefore ignored CO2 absorption by crops.

We considered various scenarios for the production of maize and soybeans for animal feed in Brazil. For the Br-CW scenario, we considered that a small part of the soybean area (and therefore also of the maize area, because a field produces both crops each year) had just been deforested the previous year. We considered that 1% of the total soybean area came from tropical rainforest and 3.4% from Cerrado. The impacts associated with this deforestation were included in the impacts of maize and soybeans from Br-CW, where CO2 emissions are the main issue. We assumed that CO2 emissions per m² of rainforest cleared are 7.5 times larger than those per m² of Cerrado cleared. Thus, based on an assumption of 74 kg CO2/m2 emitted for rainforest clearing, the value assumed for Cerrado was 9.87 kg CO2/m2 (Prudencio da Silva et al., 2010). For the Br-SO scenario and the two French scenarios (Fr-LR and Fr-ST), we assumed that crop production is not associated with any deforestation-related emissions, since deforestation in these re- gions occurred more than 20 years ago. However, the soybeans used in the French scenarios are imported from Brazil, bringing with them the emissions of the crop-production stage.

12: For the French crops (maize, wheat, rapeseed), nitrate leaching was estimated according to Basset-Mens et al. (2007). In both systems studied (Fr-ST and Fr-LR), we used a national average scenario for the production of these crops. The estimated nitrate

losses were 40, 70 and 40 kg NO3-N/ha for wheat, maize and rapeseed, respectively.

In Brazil, due to the short intervals between successive crops and the use of minimum-tillage systems and cover crops, nitrate losses are low. Based on data for soybeans in Br-SO (Basso, 2003; Moreira et al., 2004; Giacomini et al., 2007) we considered nitrate leaching to equal 20 and 25 kg NO3-N/ha for no-tillage and tillage systems, respectively. For soybeans in Br-CW, nitrate leaching was 15 and 20 kg NO3-N/ha for no-tillage and tillage systems, respec- tively. For maize in both Br-SO and Br-CW, nitrate leaching was 10 and 15 kg NO3-N/ha for no tillage and tillage systems, respectively.

losses were 40, 70 and 40 kg NO3-N/ha for wheat, maize and rapeseed, respectively.

In Brazil, due to the short intervals between successive crops and the use of minimum-tillage systems and cover crops, nitrate losses are low. Based on data for soybeans in Br-SO (Basso, 2003; Moreira et al., 2004; Giacomini et al., 2007) we considered nitrate leaching to equal 20 and 25 kg NO3-N/ha for no-tillage and tillage systems, respectively. For soybeans in Br-CW, nitrate leaching was 15 and 20 kg NO3-N/ha for no-tillage and tillage systems, respec- tively. 14: For all crops, we adopted the approach for NH3 emissions pro- posed by IPCC (2006). However, emission factors for French crops (wheat, maize and rapeseed) were based on Nemecek and Kägi (2007). NH3-N emissions were calculated by multiplying the quantity of mineral N by an emission factor specific to each type of N fertilizer (0.02 for ammonium nitrate and 0.15 for urea). For organic fertilizer (slurry), we first calculated the amount of NH3-N, which was approximately 70% of its N content. To estimate NH3-N emission at slurry application, we multiplied the amount of NH3-N by the emission factor 0.12 (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). For Brazilian crops (maize and soybeans), we adopted the same approach but updated the emission factors for organic and mineral fertilizers, for which we identified the most appropriate values based on the data for Brazil (0.25 for urea (Cantarella et al., 2008); 0.26 for organic fertilizer (Basso et al., 2004)).
15: For all crops, N20 emissions to air were estimated according to Volume 4 of IPCC (2006). We considered the N from mineral fer- tilizer and pig slurry, as well as the N from mineralization of crop residues (above and below ground). Indirect N20 emissions asso- ciated with volatilization, leaching and runoff of N inputs to managed soils were estimated according to IPCC (2006) for French crops. Emission factors proposed by Cantarella et al. (2008) and Basso et al. (2004) were used for Brazilian crops. Emissions of NO to air were estimated by multiplying the amount of N20 emitted by the emission factor 0.21 (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007).
16: Emissions of P to water were estimated according to Nemecek and Kägi (2007). The key factor to estimate the amount of P lost by erosion is the amount of soil lost during the crop cycle. For French crops (both the Fr-ST and Fr-LR systems), we used an average value valid for moderate slopes in Western Europe (0.24 t/ ha/yr) (PESERA, 2010). For Brazil, soil loss was estimated based on recommendations from EMBRAPA and literature data (Hernani et al., 1999; Cogo et al., 2003; Lima, 2005). The values assumed for the Br-CW system were 2 and 10 t/ha/yr for no-tillage and tillage systems, respectively. For the Br-SO system, the values were 1.5 and 8.0 t/ha/yr for no-tillage and tillage systems, respectively.

2: Data on GHG emissions from chicken rearing are available for France but not for Brazil; we therefore used the French values for both systems. We used data from Gac al. 4: We assumed that differences between slaughtering systems within a country were small; therefore, we collected data from one abattoir in Brazil and one in France. We assumed that the impacts of this stage were the same for both Brazilian systems; therefore, we used the same abattoir model for both Brazilian systems and the French model for the two French systems. For the transport of live chicken to the slaughterhouse, we considered the distance to be 40 km for both of the French systems, 60 km for Br-CW and 95 km for Br-SO.

Electricity use was 0.67 and 0.37 kWh/tonne of slaughtered chicken in France and Brazil, respectively. In French systems, 7 1 of fuel oil and 25.3 m³ of natural gas were consumed per tonne of slaughtered chicken. In Brazilian systems, 0.04 1 of fuel oil and 0.37 m³ of firewood were consumed per tonne of slaughtered chicken.

Packaging materials were separated into plastic (polyethylene), paper and cardboard. In France, chickens were packed in individual trays, covered with plastic wrap and an individual label. Secondary packaging consisted of a cardboard box. In Brazil, chickens were sold in two forms of transport packaging: cardboard boxes with 18- kg capacity or plastic bags with 30-kg capacity. Per tonne of slaughtered chicken, Fr-LR and Fr-ST used 10.2 kg of plastic, 2.6 kg of paper and 10.1 kg of cardboard, while Br-CW and Br-SO used 7.5 kg of plastic, 0.8 kg of paper and 12.8 kg of cardboard.

The main emissions considered for the slaughterhouse stage were biochemical oxygen demand (6.5 kg/t of slaughtered chicken in Brazil and 8.1 kg/t in France), suspended solids (7.7 kg/t in Brazil and 5.3 kg/t in France), organic substances (0.4 kg/t in Brazil and 0.2 kg/t in France) and sewage for treatment (11.4 m³/t in both countries).

9: The main functional unit (FU) adopted was 1 tonne of cooled and packaged chicken, ready for distribution, at the slaughterhouse gate. However, we also present results expressed per tonne of LW to facilitate comparison with other published results. Because Fr-LR chicken is sold at a higher price than the chicken from the other systems, we explored the use of the economic value at the farm gate as an alternative FU. Using local prices for France and Brazil, which are two separate markets, we compared the impacts per 1000 € of value for the two scenarios within each country.

To estimate this FU, we used the mean price of live chickens in 2009. The price of Fr-ST and Fr-LR chicken was 831 € and 1607 € per tonne of LW, respectively (Gallot et al., 2009), showing the higher price of the higher-quality Fr-LR chicken. In Brazil, the prices per tonne of LW were 539 € for Br-SO and 542 € for Br-CW (CONAB, 2009). These data show that the local price paid for chicken is similar for the Brazilian intensive systems (Br-SO and Br-CW), higher for the French intensive system (Fr-ST) and much higher for the French extensive system (Fr-LR).

15: <header>3.3. Contribution of life cycle stages and substances </header>

The contribution of life cycle stages to overall impacts varied according to the impact category and the production system (Table 6). We considered three main life cycle stages: i) feed pro- duction (including crop production, transport and processing into feed), ii) chicken production and iii) slaughtering (including the transport of chickens to the slaughterhouse). Impacts per kg of feed ingredient are shown in Table S3.

For acidification, the contribution of the feed-production stage exceeded 50% for Brazilian systems (51% and 55% for Br-CW and Br-SO, respectively). In contrast, the largest contribution in French systems came from the chicken-production stage (77% and 69% for Fr-LR and Fr-ST, respectively), leaving a contribution < 30% for the feed-production stage (Table 6).

For the Fr-LR system, the feed-production stage contributed 54% to eutrophication and chicken production 41% (Table 6). This contrasts with the intensive systems in Brazil, where feed pro- duction contributed 70% and chicken production contributed 23%. Fr-ST had an intermediary profile, with feed and chicken produc- tion contributing 61% and 32%, respectively. For climate change, feed production contributed 67-78%, whereas chicken production contributed 20-31%. Feed production contributed 55-73% to cu- mulative energy demand, 75-87% to terrestrial ecotoxicity and 94- 98% to land occupation.

17: <header>3.3.1. Climate change and cumulative energy demand </header>
In the four systems, CO2 contributed the most to climate change, followed by N20 and CH4 (Fig. 2).

For all systems except Br-CW, approximately 83% of CO2 emissions resulted from crop production, due to fossil fuel use and transportation between stages. Br-CW was the only system with a strong contribution (19%) of CO2 emissions resulting from defor- estation. CO2 emissions of the Br-SO system were lower than those of French systems. N20 emissions also contributed significantly to climate change, in particular from the crop production stage (70% on average), but also from the chicken house (24% on average).

Tables S4 and S5 (Supplementary materials) show the main substances and resources contributing to climate change and cu- mulative energy demand for the three major life cycle stages. Feed production contributed much to climate change, accounting for more than 67% of the total CO2eq emitted. The Brazilian systems had similar terrestrial ecotoxicity per tonne of chicken produced (9.2 and 9.4 kg 1,4-DCB eq/t). For all systems, the feed-production stage contributed most to terrestrial ecotoxicity (75-87%), due to emissions of heavy metals during production and application of fertilizers.

On average, the feed-production stage contributed 90% to land occupation (data not shown). This reflects the fact that agriculture requires more land than industrial and transport activities. Differ- ences in land occupation among the four scenarios resulted mainly from differences in feed-conversion ratios and crop yields.

27: Many studies of environmental impacts of chicken production focus on farm-specific emissions from poultry houses or litter management (De Boer et al., 2000; Ullman et al., 2004; Wheeler et al., 2006), although recent articles include impacts along the supply chain (Boggia et al., 2010; Leinonen et al., 2012). In this study, we were concerned with the fact that chicken production in chicken houses is but one step in a complex series of interlinked agricultural and industrial activities that together comprise the chicken supply chain. LCA proved to be a suitable tool for this type of global analysis.

Because chicken meat production is fundamentally dependent on a concentrated feed derived from crop production systems, transportation and processing links these two production systems together. From a life cycle perspective, upstream feed production processes are responsible for the bulk of macroscale environmental impacts associated with material and energy inputs and emissions along the chicken supply chain (Pelletier, 2008).

We confirmed these findings and realized that the strong contribution of the crop-production stage to the environmental impacts of chicken production is a determining factor, as are each system's feed-conversion rate and carcass yield at the slaughter- house. This also agrees with Williams et al. (2006), who remark that poultry and pigs consume high-value feeds and effectively live on arable land, as their nutritional needs are overwhelmingly met by arable crops that are produced both in Europe and overseas.

For the chicken production stage, the difference in feed- conversion rate between French and Brazilian systems explains some of the difference in the stage's contribution to climate change impacts (801-985 and 558-601 kg CO2eq, respectively; Table S4), but does not explain the difference between the two Brazilian systems, whose results are similar. Likewise, for the slaughter stage, the difference in carcass yield between French and Brazilian sys- tems explains that in the stage's contribution (73 and 46 kg CO2eq, respectively; Table S4). The main differences occur in contributions of the feed-production stage (2,301, 2,985, 2,147, and 1301 kg CO2eq for Fr-ST, Fr-LR, Br-CW, and Br-SO, respectively; Table S4). The lower impact of the Br-SO system is due to no deforestation im- pacts in crop production, which highlights the great influence of crop production on impacts of the chicken production chain.
One weak point of the study was a lack of replicates in the survey data and scenario modeling. Future LCA studies of chicken

production should collect multiple measures from several farms and slaughterhouses to show variability around mean impact es- timates, identify any significant differences between systems, and provide data for uncertainty analyses.

31: Chicken LW is the best FU to compare our results with those of other studies (Spies, 2003; Katajajuuri et al., 2008; Pelletier, 2008; Cederberg et al., 2009; Williams et al., 2009; Leip et al., 2010; Leinonen et al., 2012), as it is used most often in LCA studies of chicken production (Table 8).

Five authors (Spies, 2003; Katajajuuri et al., 2008; Pelletier, 2008; Williams et al., 2009; Leinonen et al., 2012) presented results for acidification, ranging from 16 to 60 kg SO2eq per of chicken LW. Our values (29-47) are between those found by Pelletier (16) and Spies (60) and agree with those found by Katajajuuri et al. (35), Williams et al. (26-31) and Leinonen et al. (33-40). Results for free range production from Leinonen et al. show that 9 kg of SO2 eq/t come from feed production, which is lower than our result for Fr-LR (15.7 kg of eq/t). As for a standard system, Leinonen et al. found 8 kg SO2 eq/ t coming from feed production, while we found 12.4 kg SO2 eq/t for Fr-ST. These differences may occur due to differing approaches (e.g., emissions factors, assumed distances). The same five authors pre- sented results for eutrophication, ranging from 2.1 to 23.0 kg PO eq/t. Our values (14-19) agree with the findings by Spies (16), Williams et al. (14-23) and Leinonen et al. (14-16). For eutrophi- cation, the results of Leinonen et al. for both free range and standard systems showed that about 50% of impact came from feed produc- tion, similar to our results. The values determined by Katajajuuri et al. (2.1) and Pelletier (3.9) are much lower than ours. All seven studies presented results for climate change, ranging from 1330 to 3437 kg CO2 eq/t. Our results (1449-2696) agree with those of Pelletier (1395), Spies (1410), Cederberg et al. (1900), Katajajuuri et al. (2079) and Williams et al. (1800 and 2000). The values deter- mined by Leip et al. (3430) and Leinonen et al. (3087-3437) are higher. For free range systems, Leinonen et al. found 2470 kg of CO2 eq/t coming from feed production, compared to 2980 kg/t in our results for the Fr-LR system. Likewise, they found 2190 kg of CO2 eq/ t coming from feed production for a standard system, while we found a little more (2300 kg CO2 eq/t) for Fr-ST and a little less (about 2000 kg CO2 eq/t) for Br-CW. The exception occurred for the standard system in southern Brazil (Br-SO), which had only 1300 kg CO2 eq/t. This occurred because we did not consider deforestation in southern Brazil. carry deforestation impacts. Three authors (Katajajuuri et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2009; Leinonen et al., 2012) presented results for land occupation, ranging from 3920 to 6700 (Williams et al.) yr/t. Our values (2465-3905 m²yr/t) are lower. Four authors (Spies, 2003; Katajajuuri et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2009; Leinonen et al., 2012) presented results for energy demand, ranging from 11.2 to 17.8 GJ/t. Our values (18.0-29.5 GJ are higher.

For acidification and eutrophication, our results were within their particularly large ranges of literature values. For land occu- pation, our values were below literature values, whereas for energy demand, our values were above them. For land occupation, although we had no access to the methodological details of the works esti- mating it, it is likely that they were based on lower yields for feed crops, resulting in greater land occupation, because crop production is the process that uses the most land in the chain analyzed. The reasons for differences in energy demand are likely to be linked to different methodological details and scope of the life cycle in- ventory, i.e., what was or was not considered as a part of the system. Some authors are particularly concerned about this (Roy et al., 2009; Flysjö, 2010; Ruviaro et al., 2012) and seek a harmonization for LCA studies in agriculture, which represents a challenge for future work.

This study has shown the major contribution of feed production to impacts (Table 6), in agreement with Pelletier (2008). However, compared to our results, Pelletier found higher contributions from feed production, accounting for 80% of energy demand, 82% of climate change, 98% of ozone depleting emissions, 96% of acidifi- cation and 97% of eutrophication for the cradle-to-farm gate pro- duction of chickens. Differing system definitions, estimates of emissions or characterization factors may have contributed to these differences. However, his work confirms the overwhelming contribution of the feed-production stage.

It was not possible to know methodological details methodol- logies for most of the other studies, but we had access to the details of the work by Spies (2003), whose estimates of field emissions from crops differed from ours. We used more recent recommen- dations from the IPCC (2006) to estimate N20 emissions and thus did not consider biologically fixed N based on a previous IPCC methodology, whereas Spies did. Consequently, our work had lower estimates for N20 emissions of soybean production. Even so, the impacts of climate change per t of live chicken are larger in our approach, due to other factors such as different amounts of inputs, diesel and limestone and the fact that we considered deforestation, whereas Spies did not. Furthermore, our estimates of NO3 emis- sions were lower, and our estimates of PO emissions were higher. In addition, energy input values of Spies were similar to ours, but their database for processes used to generate electricity seems to have excluded energy losses during transmission.

Comparison with other studies showed that our estimates of GHG emissions differ. Like Spies (2003), it seems that Pelletier (2008), Cederberg et al. (2009) and Williams et al. (2009) did not consider impacts of deforestation on crop production, and they used different approaches. Others, like Katajajuuri et al. (2008), Leip et al. (2010) and Leinonen et al. (2012) considered land use changes and probably considered impacts of deforestation on crop pro- duction from Brazil, but most likely assumed a different amount of area cleared than we did. Moreover, according to our approach, the soybean and maize produced in Brazil differ greatly according to their origin (Br-SO or Br-CW). Since we assume no deforestation in southern Brazil, our estimates of CO2 emissions are much lower for this region (Table 8 and Table S4).

carry deforestation impacts. Three authors (Katajajuuri et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2009; Leinonen et al., 2012) presented results for land occupation, ranging from 3920 to 6700 (Williams et al.) yr/t. Our values (2465-3905 m²yr/t) are lower. Four authors (Spies, 2003; Katajajuuri et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2009; Leinonen et al., 2012) presented results for energy demand, ranging from 11.2 to 17.8 GJ/t. Our values (18.0-29.5 GJ are higher.

For acidification and eutrophication, our results were within their particularly large ranges of literature values. For land occu- pation, our values were below literature values, whereas for energy demand, our values were above them. For land occupation, although we had no access to the methodological details of the works esti- mating it, it is likely that they were based on lower yields for feed crops, resulting in greater land occupation, because crop production is the process that uses the most land in the chain analyzed. The reasons for differences in energy demand are likely to be linked to different methodological details and scope of the life cycle in- ventory, i.e., what was or was not considered as a part of the system. However, compared to our results, Pelletier found higher contributions from feed production, accounting for 80% of energy demand, 82% of climate change, 98% of ozone depleting emissions, 96% of acidifi- cation and 97% of eutrophication for the cradle-to-farm gate pro- duction of chickens. Differing system definitions, estimates of emissions or characterization factors may have contributed to these differences. However, his work confirms the overwhelming contribution of the feed-production stage.

It was not possible to know methodological details methodol- logies for most of the other studies, but we had access to the details of the work by Spies (2003), whose estimates of field emissions from crops differed from ours. We used more recent recommen- dations from the IPCC (2006) to estimate N20 emissions and thus did not consider biologically fixed N based on a previous IPCC methodology, whereas Spies did. Consequently, our work had lower estimates for N20 emissions of soybean production. Even so, the impacts of climate change per t of live chicken are larger in our approach, due to other factors such as different amounts of inputs, diesel and limestone and the fact that we considered deforestation, whereas Spies did not. Furthermore, our estimates of NO3 emis- sions were lower, and our estimates of PO emissions were higher. In addition, energy input values of Spies were similar to ours, but their database for processes used to generate electricity seems to have excluded energy losses during transmission.

Comparison with other studies showed that our estimates of GHG emissions differ. Like Spies (2003), it seems that Pelletier (2008), Cederberg et al. (2009) and Williams et al. (2009) did not consider impacts of deforestation on crop production, and they used different approaches. Others, like Katajajuuri et al. (2008), Leip et al. (2010) and Leinonen et al. (2012) considered land use changes and probably considered impacts of deforestation on crop pro- duction from Brazil, but most likely assumed a different amount of area cleared than we did. Moreover, according to our approach, the soybean and maize produced in Brazil differ greatly according to their origin (Br-SO or Br-CW). Since we assume no deforestation in southern Brazil, our estimates of CO2 emissions are much lower for this region (Table 8 and Table S4).

Differing system definitions, estimates of emissions or characterization factors may have contributed to these differences. We used more recent recommen- dations from the IPCC (2006) to estimate N20 emissions and thus did not consider biologically fixed N based on a previous IPCC methodology, whereas Spies did. Consequently, our work had lower estimates for N20 emissions of soybean production. Even so, the impacts of climate change per t of live chicken are larger in our approach, due to other factors such as different amounts of inputs, diesel and limestone and the fact that we considered deforestation, whereas Spies did not. Furthermore, our estimates of NO3 emis- sions were lower, and our estimates of PO emissions were higher. In addition, energy input values of Spies were similar to ours, but their database for processes used to generate electricity seems to have excluded energy losses during transmission.

Comparison with other studies showed that our estimates of GHG emissions differ. Like Spies (2003), it seems that Pelletier (2008), Cederberg et al. (2009) and Williams et al. (2009) did not consider impacts of deforestation on crop production, and they used different approaches. Others, like Katajajuuri et al. (2008), Leip et al. (2010) and Leinonen et al. (2012) considered land use changes and probably considered impacts of deforestation on crop pro- duction from Brazil, but most likely assumed a different amount of area cleared than we did. Moreover, according to our approach, the soybean and maize produced in Brazil differ greatly according to their origin (Br-SO or Br-CW). Since we assume no deforestation in southern Brazil, our estimates of CO2 emissions are much lower for this region (Table 8 and Table S4).

40: Per tonne of whole chicken cooled and packaged, the extensive system (Fr-LR) had 1.3-1.7 times more impact than the intensive

systems because most of the environmental impacts resulted from the crop production stage. Therefore, the more grain needed to produce the same amount of live chickens, the larger the envi- ronmental impact. Fr-LR had the highest feed-conversion ratio (3.1 kg of feed per kg of chicken LW, versus approximately 1.9 for the other systems). In this case, because other stages of the life cycle did not differ much, the intensive systems required less feed per unit of animal growth, using chicken strains selected for low physical activity and concentrating the energy of ingested food into weight gain. In the extensive system, slow-growing strains were used, and the greater physical activity of the animals required more energy, resulting in a slower weight gain. Thus, a higher feed- conversion ratio engendered greater environmental impacts. Here, we found that the level of intensity clearly affected the im- pacts per unit of chicken meat produced.

Another important point is the carcass yield at slaughter, which was approximately 67% in the Fr-LR system and >70% in the other three systems. Due to the genetic strains used in the intensive systems, their carcass yield is higher than that in the Fr-LR system. This also contributed significantly to the higher environmental impacts of Fr-LR per tonne of chicken slaughtered. Therefore, im- provements in the carcass yield of the strains used in the Fr-LR system may lead to a substantial decrease in its environmental impacts.

42: While not intending to make an economic analysis, we sought to explore the fact that many French consumers are willing to pay a higher price for chicken raised in extensive systems by adopting the FU of 1000 of chicken LW at the farm gate. This change in FU often changed which system had higher impacts. For example, per tonne of LW, the Fr-LR system had 23% more climate-change impact than the Fr-ST system. Per 1000 of LW, however, the Fr-ST system had 59% more impact than Fr-LR. A similar pattern was found for en- ergy demand. We did not study why the price of chickens produced in extensive systems is higher (e.g., an indicator of consumer ex- pectations of better animal welfare, lower environmental impacts or better meat quality), but if economic value is considered as a FU of producer remuneration, the extensive system has lower impacts than the intensive systems under the LCA approach.

Therefore, determination of the FU(s) is a key element in the comparison of the two systems. It is difficult to say that one FU represents reality better than another. From the viewpoint of those involved in the production chain, comparison per tonne of LW may seem the most relevant. However, for consumers concerned about food quality, purchasing power and consumption habits, the eco- nomic FU may be more important. Consequently, a joint analysis with both FUs may be most useful, perhaps by developing a weighting method to create a unique indicator.

44: The largest contribution to environmental impacts during chicken production studied in this work came from the feed- production stage. This trend was clear when considering the pro- duction of live chickens, and the picture changed little when we added the stages of slaughter and processing. An important contribution of the feed-production stage to overall impacts, especially climate change and energy use, has been found for other animal products, such as milk (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Thomassen et al., 2008) and pork (Carlsson-Kanyama, 1998; Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005).
We found that for feed production, impacts were predominantly associated with the crop-production stage rather than with

transportation or processing stages. CO2 and N20 contributed most to climate change, NH3 and SO2 contributed most to acidification, and NH3 and NO3 contributed most to eutrophication. Production of N fertilizer also contributed strongly to climate change and en- ergy demand, in agreement with Williams et al. (2009) and Pimentel et al. (2005).

Interestingly, this study revealed that the largest contributor to acidification for the two French systems was the chicken- production stage (mainly NH3 emissions in chicken houses) rather than the feed-production stage, which was the largest contributor in the two Brazilian systems. This resulted from the use of urea as an N fertilizer for maize in Brazil. Urea has a much higher NH3-emission rate than ammonium nitrate, which is the usual N fertilizer applied on French crops. Therefore, in Brazil, the use of a more efficient N fertilizer (with less NH3 volatilization) would reduce acidification and eutrophication along the supply chain.

Of the two Brazilian systems, climate change and land occupa- tion impacts were smaller for the Br-SO system (Table 5) due to lower impacts in the crop-production stage. Feed ingredients of the Br-CW system had larger impacts than those of the Br-SO system due to recent deforestation and greater transport distances. In the French systems, soybean in the feed comes from Brazil, bringing these impacts with it. Therefore, halting deforestation for crop production in Brazil is an important step to decrease environmental impacts of poultry production.

Compared to the intensive systems, the extensive system examined in this study (Fr-LR) had higher environmental impacts per tonne produced for all impact categories analyzed. It is note- worthy that feed production for both French systems is largely the same. It is therefore important to think about ways to produce lower-impact feeds for chickens reared in extensive systems (e.g., the use of alternate ingredients, farming or other ways to produce feed with less energy consumption and fewer GHG emissions) in an attempt to decrease environmental impacts. Although not analyzed in this study, the use of organic feed ingredients, which are often less energy- and emissions-intensive due to the absence of syn- thetic fertilizers in their production (Pelletier et al., 2008), may offer a viable means of reducing the life cycle impacts of chicken pro- duction. However, Williams et al. (2006) found that, although organic field crops and animal products generally consumed less primary energy than their non-organic counterparts due to the use of legumes to fix N rather than fuel to make synthetic fertilizers, poultry was an exception, due to the high efficiency of feed con- version for non-organic poultry. Leinonen et al. (2012) found that free-range systems have similar cumulative energy demand as organic systems but higher land occupation and climate change, acidification and eutrophication impacts.

47: LCA proved to be a suitable tool for global analysis of the entire chicken supply chain. The crop-production stage is the largest contributor to overall environmental impacts along the chicken meat production supply chain. This, associated with the feed- conversion rate of each system, as well as each system's carcass yield at the slaughterhouse stage, is a determinant factor in the impacts of chicken production.

We conclude that, per tonne of chicken meat produced and for the impacts studied, the Label Rouge system causes more envi- ronmental impacts than the two systems of chicken production typical of Brazil and the standard chicken produced in France. Ef- forts to improve the feed-conversion rate and the carcass yield and to reduce the use of fossil fuels in the supply chain can help decrease environmental impacts of such extensive chicken pro- duction systems. Per tonne of chicken meat produced, the more
intensive the production system, the lower the environmental impact of the entire chain, mainly due to the more efficient feed- conversion ratio in intensive systems.

Per tonne of cooled and packaged chicken, 19% of GHG emissions of the Br-CW system came from deforestation. Using soybeans imported from Brazil in the feed of French chickens increases the climate change impact of the French chicken industry, since most soybeans imported into France come from the Br-CW region of Brazil, where deforestation occurs. To this can be added the GHG emissions of the intercontinental transport of soybeans. Therefore, the inclusion of deforestation impacts in crop production in Brazil substantially increases impacts of supply chains that use Brazilian grain, such as chicken. In southern Brazil, where deforestation no longer occurs, the potential to increase climate change impacts of chicken production is much lower. This reinforces the idea that future LCA studies that include Brazilian soybean or maize should explicitly consider their region of production in Brazil.
